did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning model combining ConvNet and DenseNet-201 with soft attention for multi-class skin lesion classification. The model was trained on the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets, achieving an accuracy of 96.13% on HAM10000 and 92.36% on ISIC.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a hybrid deep learning model (ConvNet and DenseNet-201 with soft attention) for skin lesion classification. It details specific methodology, datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC), hyperparameters, and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, F1-score) compared to baseline models, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: An Attention-based Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Skin Lesion Classification
journal: 2025 International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Communication Engineering (ECCE)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of multi-class skin lesion classification by developing a hybrid deep learning model that combines a customized ConvNet and DenseNet-201 with soft attention mechanisms. The proposed architecture fuses low-level and high-level features to improve feature learning and parameter efficiency for dermatological diagnosis. The model was trained and evaluated on two major datasets: HAM10000, containing 10,015 images, and the ISIC dataset, which provided 2,357 images. Preprocessing steps included Blackhat filtering, median filtering, digital hair removal, and data augmentation to handle artifacts and class imbalance. The system achieved an accuracy of 96.13% on the HAM10000 dataset and 92.36% on the ISIC dataset, with corresponding F1-scores of 96% and 92.37% respectively. These results demonstrate that the hybrid attention-based approach effectively classifies skin lesions with high accuracy compared to existing methods.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin lesion categories (e.g., Actinic keratosis, Basal cell carcinoma, Melanoma, Nevus, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'multi-class skin lesion classification'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories from the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC for specific lesion classes. The task involves distinguishing between distinct forms of skin lesions (benign vs malignant or specific pathology types) rather than predicting a continuous numeric outcome.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The dataset includes clinical details such as patient age, sex, and anatomical site of the lesion. Images have dimensions of 450x600 pixels with three RGB color channels.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid ConvNet and DenseNet-201 model with soft attention.
use.validation: Used for validation/evaluation of the model.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of the model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A dataset provided by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing dermoscopic images. The paper notes that augmentation was applied to this dataset.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid ConvNet and DenseNet-201 model with soft attention.
use.validation: Used for validation/evaluation of the model.
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation of the model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the performance of the multi-class classification model by visualizing the trade-off between True Positive Rate (TPR) and False Positive Rate across different thresholds.
To quantitatively analyze the classification performance, specifically to identify correct and incorrect predictions for balanced and unbalanced datasets.

statistical_methods_used.method: Per-class Precision, Recall, F1-score, and AUC
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's performance for each specific skin lesion class individually, providing a detailed breakdown of classification accuracy beyond overall metrics.
To compare the proposed hybrid model's accuracy, recall, and precision against existing methods (e.g., Inception V3, DenseNet-201, ResNet-50) and other recent studies to demonstrate effectiveness.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, precision, recall, and AUC, with comparisons made against baseline models based on these metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM-10000.notes: The proposed model achieved the best weighted accuracy of 96% compared to the baseline approach (Soumyya et al.) which had 93.4% accuracy. The model was unable to classify all classes equally due to low precision and recall for some classes like Nevus.

ISIC.notes: The suggested model achieved the greatest accuracy of 92% for the ‘ISIC-2019” dataset when compared to the results of the Soumyya et al. model [16]. The comparison’s analysis proved that, for both datasets, the suggested method performed better than other recent efforts.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Learning Model combining ConvNet and DenseNet-201 with Soft Attention

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the ISIC dataset to increase the number of images to 1000 per class. Techniques included rotation, width shift, height shift, horizontal flip, and vertical flip.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning model, combining a customized ConvNet and DenseNet-201 with soft attention, effectively classifies multi-class skin lesions by fusing low-level and high-level features to improve feature learning and parameter efficiency.

The model achieved an accuracy of 96.13% on the HAM10000 dataset and 92.36% on the ISIC dataset, outperforming baseline approaches and other recent methods such as Soumyya et al. (93.4% on HAM10000 and 90% on ISIC).

The integration of soft attention mechanisms and ensemble approaches significantly improved classification performance compared to baseline models like Inception V3, Vgg-16, and standard ConvNet or DenseNet-201 architectures.
The Adam optimizer was identified as the most effective optimizer for this task, yielding higher accuracy and precision compared to RmsProp.

While the model performs well on balanced and imbalanced datasets, it struggles to classify all classes equally due to low precision and recall for certain classes, specifically Nevus in the HAM10000 dataset.
Poor image quality remains a limitation, as it can lead to misclassification, such as confusing actinic keratosis with melanoma.
Preprocessing steps including Blackhat filtering, median filtering, digital hair removal, and data augmentation were crucial for handling artifacts and class imbalance.
important_remarks: The study is a conference paper (ECCE 2025) with limited scope compared to journal publications; external validation on independent clinical datasets is not reported.

The model exhibits significant class imbalance issues, particularly for 'Nevus' in the HAM10000 dataset, where recall dropped to 0.65 and F1-score to 0.75, indicating poor generalization for this common class.

Data leakage risk exists as the paper does not explicitly detail the train/validation/test split methodology or confirm that augmentation was applied only to the training set, which could inflate performance metrics.
The ISIC dataset used was small (2,357 images) and required augmentation to reach 1,000 images per class, limiting the robustness of the ISIC results compared to the larger HAM10000 dataset.

Performance claims are based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score without statistical significance testing (p-values), making it difficult to assess if improvements over baselines are statistically significant.
The paper acknowledges that poor image quality leads to misclassification, specifically confusing actinic keratosis with melanoma, highlighting a critical limitation for clinical deployment.
Typographical errors in class labels (e.g., 'Melonoma' instead of 'Melanoma', 'Nervus' instead of 'Nevus') in tables suggest potential data processing or reporting inconsistencies.

The comparison with existing works uses accuracy metrics from cited papers without re-running them on the same splits, which may introduce unfair comparisons due to differing preprocessing or evaluation protocols.
